# avbridge fixture corpus

A small, permanent set of media files used for end-to-end smoke testing.
Every fixture is derived from the canonical Big Buck Bunny 480p 30s MP4
(`big-buck-bunny-480p-30sec.mp4`) so the corpus is reproducible from a
single source.

## Files

| File | Container | Video | Audio | Tests |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `big-buck-bunny-480p-30sec.mp4` | MP4 | H.264 | AAC 5.1 | **native** strategy |
| `bbb-h264-aac.mkv` | MKV | H.264 (copy) | AAC 5.1 (copy) | **remux** strategy |
| `bbb-h264-aac.ts` | MPEG-TS | H.264 (copy, Annex B) | AAC (copy) | **remux** strategy with non-zero starting PTS |
| `bbb-h264-mp3.avi` | AVI | H.264 (copy) | MP3 stereo | **hybrid** strategy (libav demux + WebCodecs decode) |
| `bbb-mpeg4-mp3.avi` | AVI | MPEG-4 Part 2 (DivX 5) | MP3 stereo | **fallback** strategy (WASM software decode) |
| `failures/bbb-truncated.mp4` | MP4 (corrupt) | – | – | error path — first 80% of bytes only, no `moov` |

## Regenerating

`big-buck-bunny-480p-30sec.mp4` is the source of truth and must be checked
in by hand. Everything else is generated by `scripts/generate-fixtures.mjs`:

```bash
npm run fixtures            # generate any missing fixtures
npm run fixtures -- --force # regenerate everything
```

Requires `ffmpeg` on `PATH`.

## Running smoke tests against the corpus

```bash
# Start the demo dev server in one terminal
npm run demo

# In another terminal, run the playback smoke test
npm run test:playback -- tests/fixtures/

# Run the conversion smoke test (uses big-buck-bunny-480p-30sec.mp4 as input)
npm run test:convert
```

The playback test only walks the **top level** of the directory you pass,
so the failure fixture in `failures/` is excluded from the happy-path runs.
To verify it fails as expected:

```bash
npm run test:playback -- tests/fixtures/failures/bbb-truncated.mp4
# Expected exit code: 1
# Expected error: "<video> failed to load: PipelineStatus::DEMUXER_ERROR_COULD_NOT_OPEN"
```

## Why these specific files

Each fixture exists to exercise one path through avbridge's strategy
selection so a single smoke test run touches every backend:

- **MP4 H.264/AAC** is the trivial case — the browser plays it natively
  and avbridge gets out of the way.
- **MKV H.264/AAC** has modern codecs in a non-native container, which is
  the textbook case for the remux strategy: mediabunny demuxes the MKV and
  re-muxes to fragmented MP4 for MSE.
- **MPEG-TS H.264/AAC** is the second remux case but with two extra wrinkles:
  H.264 NAL units arrive in Annex B format (not AVCC), and the first PTS
  starts at ~1.5s instead of 0. The remux pipeline + MseSink handle both:
  mediabunny extracts the AVC config from in-band SPS/PPS, and the MseSink
  snaps `video.currentTime` to the start of the first buffered range so the
  player doesn't sit waiting for nonexistent data at t=0.
- **AVI H.264/MP3** has modern video and audio codecs in a container
  mediabunny cannot read. avbridge uses libav.js for the demux step but
  hands the H.264 packets to a WebCodecs `VideoDecoder` for hardware
  decode (and decodes the MP3 audio with libav.js for sync reasons).
- **AVI MPEG-4/MP3** has a legacy video codec with no browser decoder. The
  fallback strategy decodes both streams with libav.js in WASM.
- **Truncated MP4** is a sanity check that the player surfaces a real
  error instead of silently hanging.

The 30-second 480p clip in this directory is a trimmed excerpt used solely
for automated testing.
